html:
IV

CarlвАЩs adviser had been less efficient than HughвАЩs; therefore he knew what his courses were, where the classes met and the hours, the names of his instructors, and the requirements other than Latin for a B.S. degree. Carl said that he was taking a B.S. because he had had a year of Greek at Kane and was therefore perfectly competent to make full use of the language; he could read the letters on the front doors of the fraternity houses.

The boys found that their courses were the same but that they were in different sections. Hugh was in a dilemma; he could make nothing out of his card.

вАЬHere,вАЭ said Carl, вАЬgive the thing to me. My adviser was a good scout and wised me up. This P.C. isnвАЩt paper cutting as you might suppose; itвАЩs gym. YouвАЩll get out of that by signing up for track. P.C. means physical culture. Think of that! You can sign up for track any time tomorrow down at the gym. And E I, 7 means that youвАЩre in English I, Section 7; and M is math. You re in Section 3. Lat means Latin, of courseвБ†вАФSection 6. My adviserвБ†вАФhe tried pretty hard to be funnyвБ†вАФsaid that G.S. wasnвАЩt glorious salvation but general science. That meets in the big lecture hall in Cranston. We all go to that. And H I, 4 means that you are in Section 4 of History I. See? ThatвАЩs all there is to it. Now this thingвАЭвБ†вАФhe held up a printed scheduleвБ†вАФвАЬtells you where the classes meet.вАЭ

With a great deal of labor, discussion, and profanity they finally got a schedule made out that meant something to Hugh. He heaved a Brobdingnagian sigh of relief when they finished.

вАЬWell,вАЭ he exclaimed, вАЬthatвАЩs that! At last I know where IвАЩm going. You certainly saved my life. I know where all the buildings are; so it ought to be easy.вАЭ

вАЬSure,вАЭ said Carl encouragingly; вАЬitвАЩs easy. Now thereвАЩs nothing to do till tomorrow until eight forty-five when we attend chapel to the glory of the Lord. I think IвАЩll pray tomorrow; I may need it. Christ! I hate to study.вАЭ

вАЬMe, too,вАЭ Hugh lied. He really loved books, but somehow he couldnвАЩt admit the fact, which had suddenly become shameful, to Carl. вАЬLetвАЩs go to the movies,вАЭ he suggested, changing the subject for safety.

вАЬRight-o!вАЭ Carl put on his freshman cap and flung HughвАЩs to him. вАЬGloria Nielsen is there, and sheвАЩs a pash baby. Ought to be a good fillum.вАЭ

The Blue and OrangeвБ†вАФit was the only movie theater in townвБ†вАФwas almost full when the boys arrived. Only a few seats near the front were still vacant. A freshman started down the aisle, his вАЬbaby bonnetвАЭ stuck jauntily on the back of his head.

вАЬFreshman!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬKill him!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬMurder the frosh!вАЭ Shouts came from all parts of the house, and an instant later hundreds of peanuts shot swiftly at the startled freshman. вАЬCap! Cap! Cap off!вАЭ There was a panic of excitement. Upper-classmen were standing on their chairs to get free throwing room. The freshman snatched off his cap, drew his head like a scared turtle down into his coat collar, and ran for a seat. Hugh and Carl tucked their caps into their coat pockets and attempted to stroll nonchalantly down the aisle. They hadnвАЩt taken three steps before the bombardment began. Like their classmate, they ran for safety.

Then someone in the front of the theatre threw a peanut at someone in the rear. The fight was on! Yelling like madmen, the students stood on their chairs and hurled peanuts, the front and rear of the house automatically dividing into enemy camps. When the fight was at its hottest, three girls entered.

вАЬWimmen! Wimmen!вАЭ As the girls walked down the aisle, infinitely pleased with their reception, five hundred men stamped in time with their steps.

No sooner were the girls seated than there was a scramble in one corner, an excited scuffling of feet. вАЬIвАЩve got it!вАЭ a boy screamed. He stood on his chair and held up a live mouse by its tail. There was a shout of applause and thenвБ†вАФвАЬPlay catch!вАЭ

The boy dropped the writhing mouse into a peanut bag, screwed the open end tight-closed, and then threw the bag far across the room. Another boy caught it and threw it, this time over the girlsвАЩ heads. They screamed and jumped upon their chairs, holding their skirts, and dancing up and down in assumed terror. Back over their heads, back and over, again and again the bagged mouse was thrown while the girls screamed and the boys roared with delight. Suddenly one of the girls threw up her arm, caught the bag deftly, held it for a second, and then tossed it into the rear of the theater.

Cheers of terrifying violence broke loose: вАЬRay! Ray! Atta girl! Hot dog! Ray, ray!вАЭ And then the lights went out.

вАЬMoosick! Moosick! Moo-sick!вАЭ The audience stamped and roared, whistled and howled. вАЬMoosick! We want moosick!вАЭ

The pianist, an undergraduate, calmly strolled down the aisle.

вАЬGet a move on!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬEarn your salary!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬGive us moosick!вАЭ

The pianist paused to thumb his nose casually at the entire audience, and then amid shouts and hisses sat down at the piano and began to play вАЬLove Nest.вАЭ

Immediately the boys began to whistle, and as the comedy was utterly stupid, they relieved their boredom by whistling the various tunes that the pianist played until the miserable film flickered out.

Then the вАЬfeatureвАЭ and the fun began. During the stretches of pure narrative, the boys whistled, but when there was any real action they talked. The picture was a melodrama of вАЬlove and hate,вАЭ as the advertisement said.

The boys told the actors what to do; they revealed to them the secrets of the plot. вАЬSheвАЩs hiding behind the door, Harold. No, no! Not that way. Hey, dumbbellвБ†вАФbehind the door.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬCatch him, Gloria; heвАЩs only shy!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬNo, thatвАЩs not him!вАЭ

The climactic fight brought shouts of encouragementвБ†вАФto the villain. вАЬKill him!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬShoot one to his kidneys!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬAhhhhh,вАЭ as the villain hit the hero in the stomach.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬMuss his hair. Attaboy!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬKill the skunk!вАЭ And finally groans of despair when the hero won his inevitable victory.

But it was the love scenes that aroused the greatest ardor and joy. The hero was given careful instructions. вАЬSome neckinвАЩ, Harold!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬKiss her! Kiss her! Ahhh!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬHarold, Harold, youвАЩre getting rough!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬSheвАЩs vamping you, Harold!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬStop it; Gloria; heвАЩs a good boy.вАЭ And so on until the picture ended in the usual closeup of the hero and heroine silhouetted in a tender embrace against the setting sun. The boys breathed вАЬAhhhhвАЭ and вАЬOooohвАЭ ecstaticallyвБ†вАФand laughed. The meretricious melodrama did not fool them, but they delighted in its absurdities.

The lights flashed on and the crowd filed out, вАЬwisecrackingвАЭ about the picture and commenting favorably on the heroineвАЩs figure. There were shouts to this fellow or that fellow to come on over and play bridge, and suggestions here and there to go to a drug store and get a drink.

Hugh and Carl strolled home over the dark campus, both of them radiant with excitement, Hugh frankly so.

вАЬGolly, I did enjoy that,вАЭ he exclaimed. вАЬI never had a better time. It was sure hot stuff. I donвАЩt want to go to the room; letвАЩs walk for a while.вАЭ

вАЬYeah, it was pretty good,вАЭ Carl admitted. вАЬNope, I canвАЩt go walking; gotta write a letter.вАЭ

вАЬWho to? The harem?вАЭ

Carl hunched his shoulders until his ears touched his coat collar. вАЬGettinвАЩ cold. FallвАЩs here. Nope, not the harem. My old lady.вАЭ

Hugh looked at him bewildered. He was finding Carl more and more a conundrum. He consistently called his mother his old lady, insisted that she was a damned nuisanceвБ†вАФand wrote to her every night. Hugh was writing to his mother only twice a week. It was very confusing.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶
